attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old man presents with a three month history of left wrist pain. He cannot recall any history of injury or trauma and works as a car mechanic. He plays golf at weekends. He denies neck pain. He has no associated paraesthesia or weakness.

Examination reveals normal sensation throughout bilateral upper extremities. Neck flexion/extension/rotation does not reproduce his symptoms. Strength testing is normal in bilateral upper extremities. There is no evidence of muscle wasting on examination of the hands. With the patient making a fist surrounding the thumb, ulnar deviation of the wrist produces pain over the distal radial styloid. Prolonged wrist flexion does not elicit pain. Palpation over the flexor surface of the wrist base does not reproduce the patient's symptoms. Pain is not elicited with resisted wrist extension or flexion.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
The patient has risk factors for several causes of pain related to overuse in the upper extremity, but examination reveals a positive Finkelstein test. During the Finkelstein test, the patient was asked to make a fist with their thumb tucked inside the fingers and then bend the wrist towards the little finger (ulnar deviation). This manoeuvre reproduces pain over the distal radial styloid, which confirms a diagnosis of de Quervain tenosynovitis. The test specifically stresses the tendons of the abductor pollicis longus and the extensor pollicis brevis, which run together in a tunnel called the first dorsal compartment at the base of the thumb. If there is inflammation in this compartment, the Finkelstein test can elicit pain.

De Quervain tenosynovitis is a condition characterized by inflammation of the tendons and their sheaths on the radial (thumb) side of the wrist, resulting in pain and tenderness. It commonly occurs due to repetitive thumb movements and is often seen in individuals who perform activities such as gripping, grasping and repetitive thumb motions.

A thumb spica splint is a type of immobilizing splint that supports the thumb and wrist. It restricts movement and provides rest to the inflamed tendons in the case of de Quervain tenosynovitis. By immobilizing the thumb and wrist, the splint helps alleviate pain and reduce inflammation. This option is appropriate in this case because the patient's symptoms, such as pain over the distal radial styloid with ulnar deviation of the wrist, are consistent with de Quervain tenosynovitis.

A wrist splint is a splint that supports and immobilizes the wrist joint. Wrist splint would be helpful if the patient had carpal tunnel syndrome. While it can help with general wrist pain or injuries, it may not be as effective in specifically addressing the symptoms of de Quervain tenosynovitis, which primarily involve the thumb side of the wrist.

A counterforce forearm brace, also known as an epicondylitis brace, is commonly used for conditions like lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) rather than de Quervain tenosynovitis. Carpal tunnel corticosteroid injection is not helpful as this is not the source of his pain. It applies pressure to the forearm muscles to help relieve stress on the tendon.

A corticosteroid injection is a treatment option for de Quervain tenosynovitis when conservative measures such as splinting and activity modification have not provided sufficient relief. It involves injecting a corticosteroid medication into the tendon sheath to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms.

Paracetamol is a pain reliever that can be used for mild to moderate pain. While it can provide symptomatic relief for the patient, it is not the most appropriate next step in management for de Quervain tenosynovitis.
learningPoints: Wearing splints will assist by preventing aggravating movements of the thumb that lead to stenosis of the first dorsal compartment, facilitating rest and recovery.
references: Satteson E, Tannan SC. De Quervain Tenosynovitis. 2023 Nov 22. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan–. PMID: 28723034.

Fakoya AO, Tarzian M, Sabater EL, Burgos DM, Maldonado Marty GI. De Quervain's Disease: A Discourse on Etiology, Diagnosis, and Treatment. Cureus. 2023 Apr 24;15(4):e38079. doi: 10.7759/cureus.38079. PMID: 37252462; PMCID: PMC10208847.
Patel KR, Tadisina KK, Gonzalez MH. De Quervain's Disease. Eplasty. 2013 Jul 16;13:ic52. PMID: 23943679; PMCID: PMC3723064.

Challoumas D, Ramasubbu R, Rooney E, Seymour-Jackson E, Putti A, Millar NL. Management of de Quervain Tenosynovitis: A Systematic Review and Network Meta-Analysis. JAMA Netw Open. 2023 Oct 2;6(10):e2337001. doi: 10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.37001. PMID: 37889490; PMCID: PMC10611995.
